one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy/survey paper organizing OpenClaw-agent security threats into cognition, execution, and interaction layers and reviewing candidate countermeasures for each -- it synthesizes third-party incidents and reports rather than presenting original attacks, defenses, or experiments.
key_finding: There is no single 'finding' of the authors' own -- the paper's value is organizational/taxonomic. All concrete quantitative claims in the paper are attributed to third-party sources, not measured by the authors themselves, e.g.: Cisco reported 26% of ~31k publicly available agent skills contained at least one vulnerability; the ClawHavoc campaign (Koi Security) identified 341 malicious skills on ClawHub; the ToxicSkills study analyzed 3,984 skills and found 76 malicious payloads; a cited multi-stage compromise evaluation showed average attack success rate rising from 24.6% to 64-74% when any one state dimension (capability/identity/knowledge) is compromised; the 'Agents of Chaos' study showed a non-owner could trap two agents in a circular interaction for at least nine days, consuming about 60,000 tokens; the Clawdrain study showed a Trojanized skill increased token usage roughly six-to-seven times versus benign execution (about nine times in a more costly failure case); a Snyk report (cited in references, appearing near end of document) found prompt injection in 36% of some sampled set together with 1,467 malicious [items, text truncated in extraction].
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) that maps the security landscape of 'OpenClaw' agents -- a class of open-source, continuously-running, skill-augmented LLM agent frameworks with persistent memory and multi-channel (IM, voice, web) interaction. The paper's own contributions are: (1) an architectural breakdown of OpenClaw agents into seven components (agent-user interface, gateway control plane, agent runtime, reasoning engine, skill system, memory system, execution nodes) organized as a cognition/execution/interaction three-tier pipeline; (2) a layered taxonomy of OpenClaw-specific threats organized by cognition layer (agent goal hijack, memory & context poisoning, rogue agents), execution layer (tool misuse/exploitation, agentic supply-chain vulnerabilities, unexpected code execution, cascading failures), and interaction layer (identity/privilege abuse, insecure inter-agent communication, human-agent trust exploitation); (3) a review of existing/potential countermeasures for each layer (task goal preservation, memory/context sanitization, runtime drift monitoring, malicious tool-use chain signature libraries, supply-chain verification, sandboxing); and (4) a discussion of open research directions. The paper does not run any original experiments, attacks, or defenses -- it synthesizes and organizes threats and incidents reported elsewhere.
threat_model: Not a single formal threat model -- the paper enumerates distinct attacker capabilities per threat category. Representative assumptions drawn from the text: (a) cognition-layer -- an adversary can embed hidden/malicious instructions in external content the agent ingests (webpages, documents, tool outputs) or write poisoned entries into the agent's long-term/vector memory, without needing elevated privileges (citing Sunil et al., an ordinary query-only user can poison persistent memory); (b) execution-layer -- an adversary can publish malicious skills to the ClawHub marketplace, manipulate skill/tool descriptions to bias tool selection, or chain individually-benign tool calls into a harmful sequence (e.g., read ~/.ssh/id_rsa, compress, exfiltrate via HTTP); (c) interaction-layer -- an adversary can intercept/forge inter-agent messages (no auth/signature/encryption), replay stale authorization tokens, or exploit gateway misconfiguration (e.g., an untrusted gatewayUrl query parameter causing token leakage to an attacker-controlled endpoint, potentially escalating to RCE).
limitations: This is explicitly a survey/position paper with no original empirical validation: no new attacks are implemented or tested by the authors, no defenses are built or benchmarked, and no dataset or quantitative evaluation belongs to the authors themselves -- every number and case study in the paper is a citation to external vendor reports (Cisco, Koi Security, Trend Micro, Snyk), other academic studies, or informally-named incident reports (e.g., 'Ash's owner-spoofing incident', 'corrupted-constitution incident') whose primary sources are not independently verified within this document. 'OpenClaw' itself is described as having been publicly released in November 2025, meaning the survey covers a very young, fast-moving ecosystem; several cited defenses are described as 'potential' rather than deployed/validated. The taxonomy also overlaps substantially with general LLM-agent security surveys (the paper explicitly distinguishes itself from Deng et al., He et al., Yu et al., Wang et al., Ying et al., and Deng et al. [19] in the related-work section, but the underlying threat mechanisms -- prompt injection, memory poisoning, supply-chain attacks, tool misuse -- are largely the same classes discussed in the broader agent-security literature, adapted to OpenClaw's specific architecture).

quantitative_results: Cisco: 26% of about 31k publicly available agent skills contained at least one vulnerability (third-party citation, not authors' own measurement)
Koi Security 'ClawHavoc' campaign: identified 341 malicious skills on ClawHub (third-party citation)
'ToxicSkills' study: analyzed 3,984 skills, identified 76 malicious payloads (third-party citation)
Cited multi-stage compromise evaluation: average attack success rate rises from 24.6% to 64-74% when one of capability/identity/knowledge state is compromised (third-party citation, source [53])
'Agents of Chaos' study: a non-owner trapped two agents in a circular interaction for at least nine days, consuming about 60,000 tokens (third-party citation)
'Clawdrain' study: a Trojanized skill increased token usage about 6-7x versus benign execution, and about 9x in a costlier failure setting (third-party citation)
